_meta.coordinate_source: Wikipedia, Survey of India, Archaeological Survey of India, government tourism boards
_meta.notes: Temple sequences follow traditional/scriptural order. Distances are approximate road distances. Some temples appear in multiple circuits. All circuits reference temple IDs in mandirs.json.

pilgrimage_circuits.name: Himalayan Char Dham Yatra
pilgrimage_circuits.season_open: April/May to October/November (temples closed in winter)
pilgrimage_circuits.difficulty: Moderate to Strenuous (high altitude, treks required for 2 of 4 dhams)
pilgrimage_circuits.significance: The most important pilgrimage in Uttarakhand (DevBhumi — Land of Gods). Conceived by Adi Shankaracharya in the 8th century. Over 3 million pilgrims annually. Route runs west to east following traditional clockwise direction.
pilgrimage_circuits.permit_info: Yatra registration mandatory from 2022. Online via uttarakhandtourism.gov.in or district registrations.

temples.name: Yamunotri Temple
temples.significance: Origin of river Yamuna. First dham on the circuit. Hot spring (Surya Kund) where pilgrims cook rice and potatoes to offer to the deity.

temples.name: Gangotri Temple
temples.significance: Origin of River Ganga. Most accessible dham — road reaches temple directly. Gaumukh glacier (actual source) is 18 km further.

temples.name: Kedarnath Temple
temples.significance: Highest Jyotirlinga at 3,583 m. One of the Panch Kedars. The only Jyotirlinga in the Char Dham circuit. Temple dates to 8th century (rebuilt by Adi Shankaracharya).

temples.name: Badrinath Temple
temples.significance: Final and most important dham. Also part of Maha Char Dham and Panch Badri. Directly accessible by road. Tapt Kund (hot spring) at entrance used for ritual bathing.
nearby_attractions: Mana Village (last Indian village before Tibet)

pilgrimage_circuits.name: Maha Char Dham (Pan-India)
pilgrimage_circuits.deity_focus: Multi-deity (Vishnu/Shiva at four cardinal directions of India)
pilgrimage_circuits.difficulty: Easy to Moderate (all accessible by road or air)
pilgrimage_circuits.significance: The original Char Dham established by Adi Shankaracharya in the 8th century to unite India spiritually. Pilgrims visit one temple at each cardinal direction: North (Badrinath), East (Puri), South (Rameswaram), West (Dwarka). Completing all four is believed to grant moksha.
pilgrimage_circuits.traditional_route_order: Badrinath → Dwarka → Rameswaram → Puri (or any order, flexible)

temples.name: Badrinath Temple
temples.significance: Northern dham. Part of Himalayan Char Dham. One of 108 Divya Desams.

temples.name: Dwarkadhish Temple
temples.significance: Western dham. Legendary capital of Lord Krishna. Located at land's end in Gujarat.

temples.name: Ramanathaswamy Temple
temples.significance: Southern dham. Located on Pamban Island. World's longest temple corridor (1,200 m). Also a Jyotirlinga.

temples.name: Jagannath Temple
temples.significance: Eastern dham. Famous for world-renowned Rath Yatra chariot festival.

pilgrimage_circuits.name: 12 Jyotirlinga Circuit
pilgrimage_circuits.significance: The 12 abodes of Shiva as radiant columns of divine light (Jyoti). Mentioned in the Shiva Purana and the Dwadasha Jyotirlinga Stotra. Completing all 12 is believed to wash away all sins and grant moksha. Traditional clockwise order shown below.
pilgrimage_circuits.traditional_route_order: Somnath → Mallikarjuna → Mahakaleshwar → Omkareshwar → Kedarnath → Bhimashankar → Kashi Vishwanath → Trimbakeshwar → Vaidyanath → Nageshwar → Ramanathaswamy → Grishneshwar

pilgrimage_circuits.name: 51 Shakti Peetha Circuit (Major India-based)
pilgrimage_circuits.difficulty: Varies — some are accessible, Vaishno Devi requires 14 km trek
pilgrimage_circuits.significance: Sacred spots where body parts of Goddess Sati (Shiva's consort) fell after Lord Vishnu dismembered her body with the Sudarshana Chakra to release grief-stricken Shiva from her corpse. Each site has a presiding Shakti (goddess form) and her Bhairava (Shiva form). Considered the most powerful pilgrimage for Devi devotees.
pilgrimage_circuits.additional_peethas_note: The full 51 Shakti Peetha circuit also includes temples in Nepal (Guhyeshwari, Manasa), Bangladesh (Sugandha, Bhavani), and Sri Lanka (Mangalore). Major Indian peethas above cover the key pilgrimage sites.

temples.name: Kamakhya Temple
temples.significance: Most powerful Shakti Peetha. Womb/reproductive organ of Sati. Annual Ambubachi Mela (June) draws 500,000+ tantric sadhus and pilgrims.

pilgrimage_circuits.name: Panch Kedar Yatra
pilgrimage_circuits.difficulty: Strenuous (multiple treks of 10–24 km, high altitude)
pilgrimage_circuits.significance: Five sacred temples where the bull form of Lord Shiva (Nandi) disappeared underground after the Pandavas sought his blessings for penance after the Kurukshetra War. Each temple enshrines a different body part of Shiva. One of the most challenging and rewarding Himalayan pilgrimages.
pilgrimage_circuits.mythology: After the Kurukshetra war, the Pandavas sought Shiva's blessings for killing their kin. Shiva avoided them by hiding as a bull in the Garhwal Himalayas. When they found him, he dived underground — different body parts surfaced at five different locations.

pilgrimage_circuits.name: Panch Badri Yatra
pilgrimage_circuits.difficulty: Moderate (Bhavishya Badri requires some trek; others accessible by road)
pilgrimage_circuits.significance: Five sacred abodes of Lord Vishnu in his Badri (jujube berry) form. The Badri Kshetra (the valley of the Alaknanda river) is considered Vishnu's eternal abode. Completing all five is considered equivalent to completing all Divya Desams.

temples.name: Badri Vishal (Badrinath)
temples.significance: Main and supreme Badri. Part of both Himalayan Char Dham and Maha Char Dham.

temples.name: Bhavishya Badri Temple
temples.significance: Future abode of Badrinath — scriptures say when Joshimath's Narasimha idol loses its arm, Badrinath will shift here. Requires a 6 km trek from Tapovan.

temples.name: Vridh Badri Temple
temples.significance: Ancient form of Badrinath worshipped by sages. Lord Vishnu appeared as an old man (Vridh) here to grant blessings.

temples.name: Yogdhyan Badri Temple
temples.significance: Pandavas meditated here. Lord Vishnu is worshipped in a meditative (yogic) posture.

temples.name: Adi Badri Temple
temples.significance: Oldest and most accessible. Complex of 14 temples. Open year-round unlike Badrinath.

pilgrimage_circuits.name: Sapta Puri (Seven Sacred Cities)
pilgrimage_circuits.significance: The seven holiest cities of Hinduism (Sapta Moksha Puri — seven cities of liberation). Visiting all seven is believed to grant moksha even without completing any specific ritual. Mentioned in the Vishnu Smriti and Garuda Purana.
pilgrimage_circuits.shloka: Ayodhyā Mathurā Māyā Kāśī Kāñcī Avantikā | Purī Dvārakā caiva saptaitā mokṣadāyikāḥ ||

temples.name: Ram Mandir
temples.significance: Birthplace of Lord Rama. One of the most sacred cities in Hinduism. Now home to the newly inaugurated Ram Mandir (2024).

temples.name: Shri Krishna Janmabhoomi
temples.significance: Birthplace of Lord Krishna. One of the holiest cities. Mathura–Vrindavan is the Braj region (Krishna's childhood home).

temples.name: Mansa Devi Temple & Har ki Pauri (Ghat)
temples.significance: Gateway to the four Himalayan Dhams. Kumbh Mela every 12 years. Ganga Aarti at Har ki Pauri is one of India's most iconic rituals.

temples.name: Kashi Vishwanath Temple
temples.significance: Oldest living city in the world. Dying in Kashi grants moksha. 84 ghats line the Ganga. The city never sleeps — Ganga Aarti at Dashashwamedh Ghat (daily).

temples.name: Ekambareswarar Temple & Kamakshi Amman Temple
temples.significance: City of 1,000 temples (108 Shaivite, 108 Vaishnavite). One of the seven Mukti Kshetras. Headquarters of Shankaracharya for South India. Famous for silk weaving.

temples.name: Mahakaleshwar Temple
temples.significance: Ancient city on the Tropic of Cancer. Ujjain was once the prime meridian of ancient India. Kumbh Mela (Simhastha) every 12 years. Home of Mahakaleshwar Jyotirlinga.

temples.name: Dwarkadhish Temple
temples.significance: Legendary capital of Lord Krishna. Considered the westernmost Char Dham. The ancient city is believed to be submerged in the Arabian Sea (Dwarka Nagari).

pilgrimage_circuits.name: Ashtavinayak Yatra (Maharashtra)
pilgrimage_circuits.difficulty: Easy (all motorable, within 200 km of Pune)
pilgrimage_circuits.significance: Eight self-manifested (Swayambhu) Ganesha temples within a 300 km radius of Pune. Circuit begins and ends at Moreshwar (Morgaon). Completing the circuit is believed to fulfil all desires and cleanse sins. Traditional anticlockwise direction around Pune.
pilgrimage_circuits.traditional_route_order: Morgaon → Siddhatek → Pali → Mahad → Theur → Lenyadri → Ozar → Ranjangaon → (back to Morgaon)

temples.name: Moreshwar Temple (Morgaon)
temples.significance: First and last Ashtavinayak. The starting point of the circuit. Ganesha rides a peacock here.

temples.name: Siddhatek — Siddhi Vinayak Temple
temples.significance: Lord Vishnu worshipped Ganesha here to gain Siddhi (power) to defeat demons. The idol faces east (right side) — unique in the circuit.

temples.name: Pali — Ballaleshwar Temple
temples.significance: Only Ashtavinayak where Ganesha is named after a devotee (Ballal). Both Ganesha and Ballal's eyes are closed.

temples.name: Mahad — Varadavinayak Temple
temples.significance: The boon-granting Ganesha. An eternal lamp (Nandadeep) has been burning here continuously since 1892.

temples.name: Theur — Chintamani Temple
temples.significance: Ganesha retrieved the Chintamani gem from demon Guna for the sage Kapila. Peshwa Madhavrao spent his last days here. Highly revered by the Peshwa rulers.

temples.name: Lenyadri — Girijatmaj Temple
temples.significance: Only Ashtavinayak inside a cave (Buddhist caves complex). 283 steps to reach. Parvati performed penance here for 12 years to get Ganesha as her son.

temples.name: Ozar — Vighnahar Temple
temples.significance: Ganesha defeated the demon Vighasur here. The idol's trunk turns left (inauspicious, powerful form) — hence requires strict ritual compliance.

temples.name: Ranjangaon — Mahaganapati Temple
temples.significance: Shiva prayed to Ganesha here before his battle against Tripurasura. The idol has 10 trunks and 20 arms. A hidden chamber contains a larger, more ancient idol.

pilgrimage_circuits.name: Pancha Bhuta Stala Circuit (Tamil Nadu)
pilgrimage_circuits.significance: Five ancient Shiva temples of South India, each representing one of the five classical elements (Pancha Bhutas). Mentioned in the Thevaram (Shaiva canonical text). Completing the circuit is believed to lead to liberation from the cycle of rebirth.

temples.name: Nataraja Temple (Chidambaram)
temples.significance: Shiva worshipped as formless space. The Chidambara Rahasyam (inner sanctum secret) is a curtain representing the sky — the invisible form of Shiva.

temples.name: Jambukeshwara Temple (Thiruvanaikaval)
temples.significance: The Shivalinga is always submerged in naturally-springing water inside the Garbha Griha (sanctum). The water level never drops. One of the oldest temples in Tamil Nadu.

temples.name: Arunachaleswarar Temple (Tiruvannamalai)
temples.significance: Lord Shiva manifested as a column of fire to settle a dispute between Brahma and Vishnu. The hill Arunachala IS the Shivalinga. Deepam festival lights a fire visible for miles. Sri Ramana Maharshi's ashram is here.

temples.name: Ekambareswarar Temple (Kanchipuram)
temples.significance: Shiva worshipped as Earth element. The Shivalinga is made of sand (Prithvi). An ancient mango tree with four branches represents the four Vedas.

temples.name: Kalahasteeswara Temple (Srikalahasti)
temples.significance: The only Pancha Bhuta Stala in Andhra Pradesh. The flame inside flickers constantly (showing moving air) despite no windows or vents. Famous for Rahu-Ketu Dosha (Rahukala puja) remedies.

pilgrimage_circuits.name: Divya Desam South India Circuit
pilgrimage_circuits.significance: 108 sacred Vishnu temples glorified in the Tamil Prabandham by the 12 Alvars (Vaishnava poet-saints). First of the 108 is Srirangam, last is Tirupati. Completing all 108 is a lifetime spiritual goal for Vaishnavites.
pilgrimage_circuits.note: Full 108 Divya Desam itinerary is a lifetime circuit. Above lists the key major Desams. Tirupati (AP) and Srirangam (TN) are the most visited.

temples.name: Ranganathaswamy Temple (Srirangam)
temples.significance: One of the major Divya Desam temples in South India.

temples.name: Venkateswara Temple (Tirupati)
temples.significance: One of the major Divya Desam temples in South India.

temples.name: Padmanabhaswamy Temple
temples.significance: One of the major Divya Desam temples in South India.

temples.name: Guruvayur Temple
temples.significance: One of the major Divya Desam temples in South India.

temples.name: Badrinath Temple
temples.significance: One of the major Divya Desam temples in South India.

temples.name: Azhvar Thirunagari (Nava Tirupati)
temples.significance: One of the major Divya Desam temples in South India.

temples.name: Thirukkurungudi Temple
temples.significance: One of the major Divya Desam temples in South India.

temples.name: Nammalvar's Thiruvananthapuram (Azhwar Thirunagari)
temples.significance: One of the major Divya Desam temples in South India.

pilgrimage_circuits.name: Braj Parikrama (Mathura-Vrindavan Circuit)
pilgrimage_circuits.best_occasion: Kartik month (October–November) — lakhs do the parikrama on foot
pilgrimage_circuits.difficulty: Moderate (long foot pilgrimage; shorter Panchakroshi Parikrama in 5 days also popular)
pilgrimage_circuits.significance: The sacred land of Lord Krishna's childhood. The 84 Kos Braj Parikrama circumambulates the entire Braj Mandal covering 12 forests where Krishna played. Devotees walk the route barefoot, singing bhajans.

temples.name: Mathura — Krishna Janmabhoomi
temples.significance: Birthplace of Krishna

temples.name: Vrindavan
temples.significance: Krishna's childhood forest — the eternal Vrindavan

temples.name: Govardhan Hill
temples.significance: Hill lifted by Krishna to protect Braj from Indra's rains (Govardhan Puja)

temples.name: Barsana
temples.significance: Birthplace of Radha Rani. Famous for Lathmar Holi

temples.name: Nandagaon
temples.significance: Krishna's foster father Nanda Baba's village

temples.name: Gokul
temples.significance: Where baby Krishna was raised by Yashoda and Nanda

pilgrimage_circuits.name: Odisha Golden Triangle Pilgrimage
pilgrimage_circuits.deity_focus: Lord Jagannath (Vishnu), Lord Shiva, Lord Surya
pilgrimage_circuits.significance: Odisha's most important pilgrimage circuit combining the Maha Char Dham (Puri Jagannath), the UNESCO World Heritage Konark Sun Temple, the Temple City of Bhubaneswar, and the Chilika Lake. Best example of Kalinga architecture.

temples.name: Lingaraja Temple
temples.significance: Largest and finest temple of Bhubaneswar's 700+ temples. 11th century Kalinga architecture.

temples.name: Mukteshwar Temple
temples.significance: Gem of Odisha temple architecture (10th century). Intricate carvings. The torana (arch) at entrance is unparalleled in Indian temple art.

temples.name: Jagannath Temple (Puri)
temples.significance: Maha Char Dham. World-renowned Rath Yatra. The Mahaprasad (consecrated food) is believed to be cooked by Goddess Lakshmi herself.

temples.name: Konark Sun Temple
temples.significance: UNESCO World Heritage. The Black Pagoda. 13th century chariot of the Sun with 24 giant carved wheels.

temples.name: Sakshi Gopal Temple
temples.significance: Krishna bore witness (Sakshi) to a promise made by a devotee — a living account of divine intervention. One of the most revered Vishnu temples in Odisha.

pilgrimage_circuits.name: Six Abodes of Lord Murugan (Arupadai Veedu)
pilgrimage_circuits.significance: Arupadai Veedu refers to the six sacred temples of Lord Murugan in Tamil Nadu, India. The six temples are located in various directions representing key events in his divine life, including battles and marriage.
pilgrimage_circuits.traditional_route_order: Thiruparankundram → Tiruchendur → Palani → Swamimalai → Thiruttani → Pazhamudircholai

temples.name: Subramaniya Swamy Temple (Thiruparankundram)
temples.significance: First abode, where Lord Murugan married Goddess Deivayanai, the daughter of Indra.

temples.name: Subramaniyaswamy Temple (Tiruchendur)
temples.significance: Second abode, located on the seashore. Where he defeated the demon Soorapadman.

temples.name: Dhandayuthapani Swamy Temple (Palani)
temples.significance: Third abode. Where he renounced the world as a boy after a dispute over a divine fruit.

temples.name: Swaminatha Swamy Temple (Swamimalai)
temples.significance: Fourth abode. Where child Murugan explained the meaning of Aum to his father, Lord Shiva.

temples.name: Thiruthani Murugan Temple (Thiruttani)
temples.significance: Fifth abode. Where the Lord retired to pacify his anger after fighting demons.

temples.name: Solaimalai Murugan Temple (Pazhamudircholai)
temples.significance: Sixth and final abode, situated on a peaceful forest hill near Madurai.

pilgrimage_circuits.name: Navagraha Temples of Tamil Nadu
pilgrimage_circuits.significance: A circuit of nine ancient temples located around Kumbakonam, each representing one of the nine planetary deities in Hindu astrology (Navagrahas). Devotees visit them to resolve astrological constraints.
pilgrimage_circuits.traditional_route_order: Suryanar Kovil (Sun) → Thingaloor (Moon) → Vaitheeswaran Kovil (Mars) → Thiruvenkadu (Mercury) → Alangudi (Jupiter) → Kanjanur (Venus) → Thirunallar (Saturn) → Thirunageswaram (Rahu) → Keezhaperumpallam (Ketu)

temples.name: Suryanar Kovil (Sun)
temples.significance: Dedicated to Surya (the Sun God). Represents power, leadership, and health.

temples.name: Thingaloor Kailasanathar Temple (Moon)
temples.significance: Dedicated to Chandra (the Moon God). Governs emotions, mental peace, and motherly affection.
temples.astrological_significance: Governs emotions, mental peace, and maternal connections.

temples.name: Vaitheeswaran Kovil (Chevvai/Mars)
temples.significance: Chevvai (Mars) shrine. Shiva is worshipped here as Vaitheeswaran (Lord of Healing). Governs energy and vitality.
temples.astrological_significance: Governs courage, strength, and marital harmony.

temples.name: Thiruvenkadu Swetharanyeswarar Temple (Mercury)
temples.significance: Budha (Mercury) shrine. Governs communication, intellect, and education.

temples.name: Alangudi Apatsahayesvarar Temple (Jupiter)
temples.significance: Guru (Jupiter) shrine. Governs wisdom, spirituality, and good fortune.
temples.astrological_significance: Governs wisdom, wealth, and spiritual growth.

temples.name: Kanjanur Agneeswarar Temple (Venus)
temples.significance: Sukran (Venus) shrine. Governs arts, relationships, wealth, and beauty.
temples.astrological_significance: Governs arts, beauty, relationship, and fine life.

temples.name: Thirunallar Dharbaranyeswarar Temple (Saturn)
temples.significance: Shani (Saturn) shrine. Highly popular, believed to alleviate karmic challenges.
temples.astrological_significance: Governs discipline, longevity, and karmic lessons.

temples.name: Thirunageswaram Naganathar Temple (Rahu)
temples.significance: Rahu shrine. Worshipped to overcome obstacles and gain spiritual growth.
temples.astrological_significance: Governs desires, worldly success, and occult knowledge.

temples.name: Keezhaperumpallam Naganathaswamy Temple (Ketu)
temples.significance: Ketu shrine. Governs wisdom, detachment, and spiritual liberation (Moksha).
temples.astrological_significance: Governs liberation (Moksha), detachment, and spiritual depth.

pilgrimage_circuits.name: Pancha Bhuta Sthala (Five Elements Circuit)
pilgrimage_circuits.significance: Circuit of five ancient temples representing the five elements of nature (Earth, Water, Fire, Air, and Space). Promoted by Adi Shankaracharya to show the omnipresence of Shiva in nature.

temples.name: Ekambareswarar Temple (Earth)
temples.significance: Represents the Earth (Prithvi) element. The main lingam is made of sand.

temples.name: Jambukeswarar Temple (Water)
temples.significance: Represents the Water (Appu) element. Underground springs keep the sanctum wet.

temples.name: Arunachaleswarar Temple (Fire)
temples.significance: Represents the Fire (Agni) element, located at the base of the sacred Annamalai Hill.

temples.name: Kalahasteeswara Temple (Air)
temples.significance: Represents the Air (Vayu) element. Lingam is worshipped as wind.

temples.name: Nataraja Temple (Space)
temples.significance: Represents the Space (Akasha) element. The deity is represented in cosmic dance form.

pilgrimage_circuits.name: Pancharama Kshetras of Andhra Pradesh
pilgrimage_circuits.significance: Five sacred Shiva temples in Andhra Pradesh. The Shiva Lingas here are believed to be parts of a single giant Shiva Linga owned by the demon Tarakasura, which shattered into five pieces during a celestial battle.

temples.name: Amararama Temple
temples.significance: First Aramam, located on the bank of Krishna river. Features a high marble Linga.

temples.name: Somarama Temple
temples.significance: Second Aramam, where the Linga changes color with phases of the moon.

temples.name: Ksheerarama Temple
temples.significance: Third Aramam. Worshipped as the milk-coloured Linga.

temples.name: Draksharama Temple
temples.significance: Fourth Aramam, known as Dakshina Kashi. Historic Chalukyan carvings.

temples.name: Kumararama Temple
temples.significance: Fifth Aramam. A monolithic Linga rising to the upper level of the sanctum.

pilgrimage_circuits.name: Ahobilam Nava Narasimha Circuit
pilgrimage_circuits.significance: The sacred forest valley of Ahobilam where Lord Narasimha manifested. Features nine temples displaying nine different forms of Narasimha, split between Lower and Upper Ahobilam.
pilgrimage_circuits.traditional_route_order: Bhargava → Yogananda → Chatravata → Ahobila → Kroda → Karanja → Malola → Jwala → Pavana

temples.name: Bhargava Narasimha Temple
temples.significance: Worshipped by Sage Parashurama.

temples.name: Yogananda Narasimha Temple
temples.significance: Post-battle peaceful yoga posture.

temples.name: Chatravata Narasimha Temple
temples.significance: Depicts a smiling Narasimha under a Peepal tree.

temples.name: Ahobila Narasimha Temple
temples.significance: Main cave temple where self-manifested deity resides.

temples.name: Kroda Narasimha Temple
temples.significance: Varaha and Narasimha combination form.

temples.name: Karanja Narasimha Temple
temples.significance: Under Karanja tree, holding a bow and arrow.

temples.name: Malola Narasimha Temple
temples.significance: Peaceful form sitting with Goddess Lakshmi.

temples.name: Jwala Narasimha Temple
temples.significance: Spot where Lord Narasimha defeated Hiranyakashipu.

temples.name: Pavana Narasimha Temple
temples.significance: The most remote forest temple.

pilgrimage_circuits.name: Great Living Chola Temples Circuit
pilgrimage_circuits.significance: UNESCO World Heritage group of three stunning 11th-12th century temples built by the Chola Empire, celebrating the peak of Dravidian architecture.
pilgrimage_circuits.traditional_route_order: Thanjavur Brihadeeswarar → Gangaikonda Cholapuram → Darasuram Airavatesvara

temples.name: Brihadeeswarar Temple (Thanjavur)
temples.significance: UNESCO site built by Raja Raja Chola I. Features a monumental 66m stone tower.

temples.name: Brihadeeswarar Temple (Gangaikonda Cholapuram)
temples.significance: Built by Rajendra Chola I. Houses the largest stone Shiva Linga in South India.

temples.name: Airavatesvara Temple (Darasuram)
temples.significance: UNESCO site built by Raja Raja Chola II. Shaped like a stone chariot.

pilgrimage_circuits.name: Himalayan Panch Prayag Yatra
pilgrimage_circuits.significance: A sacred circuit of the five holy confluences (Prayags) of the Alaknanda River in the Garhwal Himalayas. Each confluence represents a highly sacred geographic and spiritual junction where major rivers merge to form the Ganges.

temples.name: Raghunathji Temple (Devprayag)
temples.significance: Confluence of Alaknanda and Bhagirathi rivers, where the Ganges officially begins.

temples.name: Chamunda Devi Temple (Rudraprayag)
temples.significance: Confluence of Alaknanda and Mandakini rivers.

temples.name: Uma Devi Temple (Karnaprayag)
temples.significance: Confluence of Alaknanda and Pindar rivers.

temples.name: Gopalji Temple (Nandaprayag)
temples.significance: Confluence of Alaknanda and Mandakini rivers.

temples.name: Laxmi Narayan Temple (Vishnuprayag)
temples.significance: Confluence of Alaknanda and Dhauliganga rivers.

pilgrimage_circuits.name: Hoysala Heritage Trail
pilgrimage_circuits.significance: A heritage circuit covering the magnificent star-shaped soapstone temples built by the Hoysala Empire. These UNESCO World Heritage Sites are celebrated worldwide for their unmatched stone carving precision.
pilgrimage_circuits.traditional_route_order: Chennakeshava Temple (Belur) → Hoysaleswara Temple (Halebidu) → Keshava Temple (Somanathapura)

temples.name: Chennakeshava Temple (Belur)
temples.significance: Famous for its ornate Dravidian-Hoysala facade and standard soapstone madanika figurines.

temples.name: Hoysaleswara Temple (Halebidu)
temples.significance: UNESCO World Heritage Site with spectacular twin shrines and massive monolith Nandi statues.

temples.name: Keshava Temple (Somanathapura)
temples.significance: A beautifully complete star-shaped triple-shrine (Trikuta) temple dedicated to Lord Vishnu.

pilgrimage_circuits.name: Mewar Forts and Shrines Circuit
pilgrimage_circuits.significance: Covers two highly revered historic temples of the Mewar region which were patronized by Mewar royalty and continue to serve as major centers of devotion.

temples.name: Dwarkadhish Temple (Kankroli)
temples.significance: A key shrine of the Pushtimarg sect located next to the Rajsamand Lake.

temples.name: Charbhuja Temple (Garhbor)
temples.significance: Historic fortress temple dedicated to a four-armed form of Lord Vishnu.

pilgrimage_circuits.name: Goa Temple Trail
pilgrimage_circuits.significance: A scenic circuit showcasing the unique Goan-Portuguese fusion temple architecture and the ancient basalt rock temples nestled in the Western Ghats.
pilgrimage_circuits.traditional_route_order: Mangueshi Temple → Shanta Durga Temple → Tambdi Surla Mahadev Temple

temples.name: Mangueshi Temple (Priol)
temples.significance: Features a unique 7-story lamp tower (Deepasthambha) and stunning dome architecture.

temples.name: Shanta Durga Temple (Kavalem)
temples.significance: Dedicated to the peaceful form of Goddess Durga mediating between Shiva and Vishnu.

temples.name: Tambdi Surla Mahadev Temple
temples.significance: The oldest surviving stone temple in Goa, built of black basalt in Kadamba style.

pilgrimage_circuits.name: Vrindavan Dham Parikrama
pilgrimage_circuits.significance: A devotional loop of the historic temples established by the Goswamis of Vrindavan. Pilgrims complete this trail to experience the core land of Lord Krishna's childhood pastimes.
pilgrimage_circuits.traditional_route_order: Bankey Bihari Temple → Radha Raman Temple → Radha Vallabh Temple → Radha Damodar Temple

temples.name: Bankey Bihari Temple (Vrindavan)
temples.significance: One of the most famous Krishna temples where the deity is worshipped with a curtain-based darshan.

temples.name: Radha Raman Temple (Vrindavan)
temples.significance: Houses a self-manifested deity from a Saligram stone. The kitchen fire has burned for 480+ years.

temples.name: Radha Vallabh Temple (Vrindavan)
temples.significance: Deity is represented by a golden crown alongside Radha.

temples.name: Radha Damodar Temple (Vrindavan)
temples.significance: Houses the holy Govardhan Shila displaying the footprint of Lord Krishna.

pilgrimage_circuits.name: Kashi Guard and Shakti Trail
pilgrimage_circuits.significance: A local trail in Varanasi that connects the major guardian and Shakti temples of the city. Worshippers visit these to seek protective blessings and spiritual power.
pilgrimage_circuits.traditional_route_order: Kaal Bhairav Temple → Sankat Mochan Hanuman Temple → Durga Mandir

temples.name: Kaal Bhairav Temple (Varanasi)
temples.significance: Dedicated to the spiritual police guardian (Kotwal) of Kashi.

temples.name: Sankat Mochan Hanuman Temple (Varanasi)
temples.significance: Established by saint poet Tulsidas, highly popular Hanuman shrine.

temples.name: Durga Mandir (Varanasi)
temples.significance: An 18th-century red sandstone temple displaying classical Nagara Shikhara design.

pilgrimage_circuits.name: Gujarat Sacred Coast Circuit
pilgrimage_circuits.significance: A coastal route linking the highly famous Shiva and Krishna shrines along the Saurashtra and Kathiawar coastline, combining Jyotirlinga and oceanic pilgrimage spots.
pilgrimage_circuits.traditional_route_order: Somnath Temple → Bhalka Tirth → Dwarka Bhadkeshwar Mahadev → Nishkalank Mahadev → Gopnath Mahadev

temples.name: Somnath Temple
temples.significance: The first among the twelve Jyotirlinga shrines of Lord Shiva, sitting directly on the beach.

temples.name: Bhalka Tirth (Veraval)
temples.significance: The holy site where Lord Krishna left the earthly plane.

temples.name: Dwarka Bhadkeshwar Mahadev Temple
temples.significance: A gorgeous coastal temple situated on a hillock in the Arabian Sea near Dwarka.

temples.name: Nishkalank Mahadev Temple (Koliyak)
temples.significance: A unique sea temple where lingams emerge during low tide, allowing devotees to walk to them.

temples.name: Gopnath Mahadev Temple (Bhavnagar)
temples.significance: Coastal temple where Narsinh Mehta received a vision of Krishna's Raas Leela.
